title: Over 200 million litres of extra diesel coming to Australia
text:
E﻿xtra diesel fuel is on the way to Australia from Asian suppliers, the prime minister has announced.

Anthony Albanese said BP and Viva had each secured two addi﻿tional cargoes, with all four adding up to just over 200 million litres.

The purchases were made with the support of Export Finance Australia. The additional supplies will arrive in either the last week of May or the first week of June.

LIVE UPDATES: ﻿ Trump announces ceasefire extension with Iran

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese. (9News)

The NRMA says Australia, on average, uses about 93 million litres of diesel a day.

The 200 million litre supply the PM spoke of adds around two and a half day's worth of supply - though as he emphasised, this is "additional" supply on top of what's been contracted.

Two of the extra cargoes were purchased from South Korea, one from Brunei, and one from Malaysia.

﻿"We will continue to use every measure at our disposal to make a difference," Albanese said.

Albanese said the government continued to work with fuel and fertiliser﻿ suppliers to secure extra cargoes for Australia.

The national cabinet is set to meet this week, though Albanese would not be drawn on the specific topics that would be discussed.

﻿"It's important that state and territories be informed and it is important that we work together as a nation in what is a very challenging time for the whole world," he said.

Albanese also refused to commit﻿ to whether or not the government would extend the current fuel excise cut.

The cut is currently in place until the end of June, and Albanese said the volatility of the ongoing conflict in the Middle East﻿ made it impossible to predict at this point whether an extension would be necessary.

"This is a volatile, turbulent period in the world," he said.﻿

title: UK to introduce lifelong smoking ban for anyone born after 2008
text:
Children aged 17 and under will be banned from ever legally purchasing cigarettes in their lifetime under new legislation in Britain.

The legislation, which cleared both the House of Commons and the House of Lords this week, creates a "smoke-free generation" by making it illegal for retailers to sell tobacco to anyone born after 2008.

Once the bill receives Royal Assent, British ministers will also wield significant new powers to regulate the flavours, packaging, and display of vaping and nicotine products to make them less appealing to minors.

Children born after 2008 will be banned from buying cigarettes for life in the UK. (Supplied)

The BBC reports that the bill is a cornerstone of a broader strategy to tackle smoking, which remains the UK's leading cause of preventable death and disability.

Health Secretary Wes Streeting hailed the vote as an historic moment for the country.

He stated that prevention is better than cure and that this reform will save lives while easing pressure on the NHS to build a healthier Britain.

In the House of Lords, Health Minister Baroness Merron echoed this sentiment during the final stages of the debate on Monday.

She told peers that it is the biggest public health intervention in a generation and assured all noble Lords that it will save lives.

New restrictions on vaping

While the headline focus is the generational tobacco ban, the laws also tighten the screws on vaping.

Use of e-cigarettes will now be banned in cars carrying children, in playgrounds, outside schools, and at hospitals.

However, the laws stop short of a total outdoor ban, as pub gardens, beaches, and private residences are currently excluded from the new restrictions.

Vaping would also still be allowed outside hospitals in a bid to support those trying to quit.

This approach met with some pushback during the parliamentary process. Lord Naseby, a Conservative peer, noted that the bill does upset a great many people in that industry, including small retailers.

He argued that what is really needed is a proper understanding of how to educate people not to take up smoking.

Baroness Merron responded by assuring Lord Naseby that the government has worked closely with retailers and will continue to do so.

How it compares to Australia

While the UK's "sliding age" tobacco ban is globally unique, its approach to vaping remains significantly more relaxed than the stringent regime currently in place in Australia.

Here, the federal government has effectively moved to end the retail sale of all vapes outside of a clinical setting.

In Australia, vapes are treated strictly as therapeutic goods rather than consumer products. It is illegal for any non-pharmacy retailer to sell vaping products, and the country has implemented a total ban on the importation of disposable, single-use vapes regardless of nicotine content.

While the UK is just now gaining the power to regulate flavours, Australia has already restricted them to tobacco, menthol, and mint to strip away the "candy-coated" appeal to children.

Australia has also long sought to discourage cigarette smoking through some of the world's highest taxes, with a standard pack now often exceeding $50.

However, critics say these dual pressures - sky-high prices and strict anti-vaping measures - have allowed black market products to flourish.

A call for industry accountability

Advocacy groups have welcomed the UK's progress but are already calling for more.

Sarah Sleet from Asthma + Lung UK said the legislation promised to transform the nation's health.

She noted that now that the groundbreaking bill is finally over the line, there is a chance to go further to protect public health and hold the tobacco industry to account.

"Right now we have a postcode lottery, which is why the tobacco industry should pay through a levy to reduce the harm they cause by funding these crucial services across the UK," she told the BBC.

title: Alarming number of Aussies chasing mates for money, figures reveal
text:
Nearly two million people are owed money by a mate – and for many, it's not a small amount.

New research from financial comparison site Finder shows that almost one in ten Aussies are short-changed by a friend who hasn't repaid them.

The average owing sits at a staggering $1855, suggesting these aren't minor, one-off occurrences.

LIVE UPDATES: Trump declares extension of ceasefire

Experts say outstanding balances among friends can cause friction. (Getty Images/iStockphoto)

Group gifts, restaurant bills, shared Ubers, event tickets and travel costs are among the repeat offenders.

Younger Australians are the most likely to be left in the lurch. The survey found that 12 per cent of Gen Z respondents are owed money, compared to 10 per cent of millennials and 8 per cent of Gen X.

Money expert Ceyda Erem said that while payment apps make it easier to settle up quickly and prevent one person from being left to pay, outstanding balances among friends can still cause friction.

The average amount owed by a friend sits at a whopping $1855. (Getty)

"While most debts between friends are relatively small, they can still add up and create frustration."

Etiquette expert Jo Hayes said to avoid an uncomfortable situation down the track, pay someone back straight away.

"Electronic bank transfers mean there's really no excuse for being tardy. Get their bank details and transfer the cash pronto.

"Don't put your friends and family in the awkward position of needing to chase you for the money."

For those wanting to recoup their losses, addressing the issue head on is the best place to start.

"The first step is to directly ask for repayment. It might feel uncomfortable, but avoiding the conversation can lead to resentment over time," Erem said.

Erem explained that before offering to shout a meal for the table, it's important to consider your financial standing.

"Money is tight, and many simply can't afford to let these costs slide … it's important to make sure you're not putting your own financial wellbeing at risk."

title: Neil wants pokies machines to play 'losing sounds'. Here's why
text:
During a Christ﻿mas lunch with mates, Neil Walshe reluctantly shuffled into a pokies room.

Nothing was amiss: Flashing lights and high-pitched slot machine jingles are a common backdrop to pub meals in Australia.

But then came a startling realisation.﻿

"As they always do, my mates say, 'We're going to go and have a slap', and so I walked in the room with them," Walshe recalled to Nine.com.au.

Neil Walshe is advocating for losing sounds to be installed in pokies machines. (Supplied)

Do you have a story? Contact reporter April Glover at april.glover@nine.com.au

"They're gambling away and I'm just trying to entertain myself.

"And I realised then and there that I couldn't hear any losing sounds in the whole room."

He was right –﻿ there are hundreds of thousands of poker machines in operation in Australia.

Almost none of them have a specific sound to indicate when money is lost.

Instead, gamblers are injected with an artificial dopamine hit thanks to the sound of winning chimes at the end of a game. Or there is no sound at all.

It is something addiction experts have long described as "losses disguised as wins".﻿

"I might bet a dollar, win back 20 cents. But nonetheless, the machine plays a sound effect, a positive sound effect," Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the Melbourne School of Psychological Sciences, Dan Myles, explained.

"It also plays the kinds of animations that you would typically see following a genuine win.

"The research has shown us that people do tend to overestimate how often they think they're winning when we include these events in a gambling session, relative to when we remove them."

Walshe, pictured with his daughter, conducted his own research on how losing sounds impact casual gamblers. (Supplied)

Walshe was determined to prove that the absence of loss sounds was stirring up addiction inside even casual gamblers.

He took the matter into his own hands and conducted research into the impact of losing sounds.

He bought a poker machine, rigged it to play a specific sound when players lost, and tested it on a group of casual players.

A sound engineer created the losing﻿ "bleep" in a descending minor key, similar to the sound you hear if you die in a video game.

Tampering with ﻿a poker machine is technically illegal in most states and territories.

Walshe﻿ risked a hefty fine or jail but he said it was for a "good cause".

"I wanted to take away this dopamine hit that seems to be dished out no matter whether you win or lose," he explained.

Walshe is no addiction expert but he said the results speak for themselves.﻿

"I wanted to take away this dopamine hit that seems to be dished out no matter whether you win or lose."

"The small-scale test proved when I tested it on these gamblers was that they didn't actually like hearing the losing sound," he said.

"And every time it went off, they would reassess how much money they had, and they would think twice about putting more money in.

"From what I saw with our experiment, it seriously slowed people down."

He bought a poker machine, rigged it to play a specific sound when players lost, and tested it on a group of casual players. (Supplied)

Myles agrees that the addition of losing sounds would "balance the scales" for players who might mistakenly inflate their wins.

He said animations and sounds contribute to a deep state of psychological immersion in gaming machines, which can lead to addiction.﻿

"We are doing research that's looking into what manipulating these features might do, and how we could maybe better regulate these machines to make them less misleading," Myles added.

Australians lose $31.5 billion to the gambling industry each year, according to a 2025 report released by Equity Economics.

The average loss on a pokies machine amounts to more than $1950 per person.

How one family was left devastated by pokies addiction﻿

Brad* ﻿was blindsided years ago when his wife secretly gambled away $150,000 on the pokies.

He told Nine.com.au his world came crashing down when he applied for a home loan.

"I should have had about $60,000 [in a term deposit]. They told me there was only an account with $4.21 in it," Brad said.

"Initially I thought... we've been scammed."﻿

"But there had been an ongoing addiction unknown to me for probably five or six years."

His relationship ended and his family was left irreparably broken by the pokies addiction.

"The kids' inheritance had gone... The Mastercard had been maxed out," he said.

"I just never bounced back financially."

Walshe is no addiction expert but he said the results speak for themselves.﻿ (Supplied)

The fight to change the law﻿

Brad said he passionately supported Walshe's ambitious﻿ attempt at changing the law.

And Walshe knows he is facing ﻿a David versus Goliath battle by squaring up against a $12 billion industry.

He hopes that one small law change like adding losing sounds will make a big difference.

A Change.org petition has been set up to call on﻿ gaming authorities to change the design of machines.

﻿There is some progress being made in gambling law reform after the federal g﻿overnment announced sweeping changes to betting advertising would come into effect.

And other anti-gambling advocates have called for loss limits on pokies machines to be enforced in Australia.

"Laws can change overnight if they want it to happen," Walshe said.

"We saw it during COVID. ﻿

"Even if it takes years pushing this ... ﻿the losing sound might just be another little lever that helps more gambling reform happen."

*Not his real name.﻿

There are many different ways to get help and information about gambling.

You can visit the National Gambling Helpline or call on 1800 858 858.Readers seeking support can contact Lifeline on 13 11 14 or beyond blue on 1300 22 4636.

title: Millions of dollars in AI camera fines wiped
text:
Thousands of West Australian drivers have successfully appealed artificial intelligence -based camera infringements, wiping $2.2 million in fines.

But as pressure mounts to press pause on the controversial cameras , the government says it's going to roll out even more.

Over the past six months more than 54,000 infringements have been issued to WA drivers for incorrectly wearing a seatbelt or not wearing one at all.

Thousands of WA drivers have successfully appealed AI camera infringements, wiping $2.2 million in fines. (9News)

The acts have been caught by AI cameras, with new data revealing nearly $10 million in fines have been issued since they officially launched in October.

Perth father Steven Shaw is one of those fined.

Over a two-month period, between December and January, his passenger was caught four times by AI cameras improperly wearing her seatbelt on the freeway.

"When you're driving 100[km/h] down the freeway, there's no safe way you can safely monitor a passenger's seatbelt location without potentially causing an accident," Shaw told 9News.

As the driver, Steve Shaw was hit with more than $2000 in fines and 20 demerits, starting a four-month battle and putting his livelihood at stake. (9News)

But, as the driver, he was hit with more than $2000 in fines and 20 demerits, starting a four-month battle and putting his livelihood at stake.

"There was two or three weeks where I didn't know if I'd lost my licence, I was in limbo and my wife driving much of the time," Shaw said.

Shaw challenged his infringements, with the Department of Transport withdrawing all but the first one, which he'll contest in court.

The father is one of 2800 drivers who've successfully appealed their infringement notices, wiping $2.2 million in fines.

"AI cameras in my opinion are not suitable for this form of infringement, they are for speeding and phones," Shaw said.

The acts have been caught by AI cameras, with new data revealing nearly $10 million in fines have been issued since they officially launched in October. (9News)

WA Premier Roger Cook said it's not the AI cameras' fault but that the driver gets clocked multiple times in a short window.

"People are often getting penalised once, twice or three times before they're even aware that a fine has been raised in their name," Cook said.

Despite the AI camera backlash, the WA government plans to roll out even more within the next six months.

The opposition says the government needs to iron out the issues before launching more.

"The government haven't gotten it right," Opposition Leader Basil Zempilas said.

"It needs to be tweaked and they need to get it right before more fines are issued."

title: Group says site of new Orange greyhound track should be for housing
text:
A new greyhound racing track in regional New South Wales has been approved, despite council and community opposition.

The Western Regional Planning Panel (WRPP) gave the green light to a $15 million development application for a racing facility in Orange on Tuesday.

The unanimous decision was handed down a week after more than a dozen opponents fronted the panel to argue against the plan citing concerns about animal welfare and gambling.

Among them was former Greens councillor Neil Jones who heads the Community Before Greyhound Tracks group.

He said a petition urging the council to oppose the plan gathered more than 1,100 signatures.

"I'm very disappointed, very angry," Mr Jones said.

" I think the planning panel has made a wrong decision, and the decision is not in the public interest. "

Orange's former horse trotting track has been given approval to be redeveloped into a greyhound racing facility. (ABC Central West: Xanthe Gregory)

The NSW Greyhound Breeders, Owners and Trainers Association (GBOTA) project involves redeveloping the former Orange Harness Racing (trotting) Track on the city's south-eastern fringe which has sat unused for more than a decade.

The Orange City Council sold the land to GBOTA in 2023.

But just days before the panel was set to make a decision in mid-April councillors voted to oppose the DA.

Mayor Tony Mileto said some believed the project failed to deliver what was initially promised — a state-of-the-art track and function centre that would attract large crowds.

"It falls quite short of what the community was expecting, of what some councillors were expecting," he said.

The approved plans include a small club house, seating for up to 64 people, 112 race day holding kennels and a soccer field in the middle of the track.

The WRPP heard that between 80 to 100 punters were expected at its weekly race meetings.

Cr Mileto said he was "not surprised" the project was approved, but questioned the GBOTA's claim that the facility would inject $19 million a year into the region's economy.

"I struggle to see how that's going to occur," he said.

GBOTA's chief operations officer Jason Lyne told the planning panel the proposal was about "revitalisation" and bringing new life to the unused trotting track.

"Rather than allowing this land to sit idle we have a genuine opportunity to transform it into a vibrant, multi-purpose asset," he said.

"This proposal represents an opportunity to create something lasting, something that supports sport, strengthens community connections and drives economic growth."

Mr Lyne said the project had attracted strong support from the city's business community and would bring visitors to the region.

He said the development application met all planning requirements and a council staff report recommended it for approval.

'Lost opportunity'

But Mr Jones said the decision to approve the track was "an economic loss to Orange".

He said the 16-hectare site was earmarked by the council in 2022 as potentially suitable for housing development to address an ongoing supply shortage in Orange.

"[It's] 16 hectares of land, very valuable, council-owned on the edge of the strategic gateway to Orange which the previous council reportedly sold for $1.6 million," Mr Jones said.

" It's worth ten times that. "

Neil Jones from the Community Before Greyhound Tracks group with wife Libby and their rescue greyhound Sammy. (Supplied: Carla Freedman)

Mr Jones said the region was in far greater need of affordable housing than a sport that has been banned in the ACT.

In NSW, seven tracks including Tamworth, Wauchope, Young, Kempsey and Moree closed at the end of 2025.

Racing also recently ceased at Dapto, and Muswellbrook, Wagga Wagga and Broken Hill are slated for closure.

"We're alienating [the land] to a dying industry," Mr Jones said.

"[It's] such a lost opportunity for the city of Orange to do something that benefits the whole community," he said.

The site on the fringe of Orange has sat unused for more than a decade. (ABC Central West: Xanthe Gregory )

In its determination, the panel said it "does not seek to downplay" the matter of greyhound welfare that was raised as a "consistent concern" by members of the public, as well as the impacts of gambling.

But it said those matters did not fall within its legislative considerations of development applications.

"The panel acknowledges the link between greyhounds and the proposed development. However, a separate Act of Parliament specifically addresses the matter of greyhound welfare," the determination report said.

" Equally, the panel understands the concerns expressed regarding the impact of gambling on the social welfare of the community. However, this is a matter for legislative consideration. "

Despite the development application receiving the tick of approval, it remains to be seen whether the track will be built.

One of the conditions imposed by the council at the time of sale required GBOTA to enter into a contract with a builder to complete the project within two years.

title: Former minister threatened developers over working with CFMEU, inquiry told
text:
Former Queensland Labor Minister Grace Grace allegedly threatened developers behind the $1.6 billion Toowoomba Second Range Crossing project, saying she would tear up their contract unless they worked with the CFMEU.

The former minister for industrial relations allegedly made the comments after a series of safety issues at the 41km construction site in February 2018, the Commission of Inquiry into the CFMEU has heard.

Construction on the road, which created a major bypass of Toowoomba for freight and traffic heading west on the Warrego Highway, began in 2016 and concluded in 2019.

There were several bridges that had to be built, as well as an 800 metre viaduct and a 30m deep cutting through volcanic rock.

Senior counsel assisting Patrick Wheelahan told the inquiry there were 17 incidents on the construction site where plant machinery rolled over.

Patrick Wheelahan says the CFMEU was seeking to gain a foothold on the Toowoomba Second Range Crossing project. (ABC News)

He said the developer Nexus, a consortium made up of a range of companies including ACCIONA and Ferrovial Argoman, understood the project had its difficulties, which were attributable to the "difficult terrain this project had to be delivered in".

The companies had an enterprise agreement with the Australian Workers Union.

"The CFMEU were seeking to gain a foothold on this project, using its regulatory capture of Workplace Health and Safety Queensland and taking advantage of ministerial threats to this project," Mr Wheelahan said.

Mr Wheelahan said from July 2017 to February 2028 there were 74 improvement notices issued by WHSQ inspectors against the project, while in the eight months prior, there were only 16.

"Between [February 9, 2018] and [February 19], the CFMEU issued 20 entry notices … those notices were seeking to enter the site to investigate alleged health and safety contraventions," he said.

Construction on the Toowoomba bypass began in 2016 and concluded in 2019. (ABC News: Scout Wallen)

But Mr Weelahan said the reasons given by the union were vague, describing reasons such as "access and egress to all work areas" and "traffic control".

Nexus, he said, made the decision to challenge those notices in the Queensland Industrial Relations Commission.

The inquiry heard that on February 21, 2018, a section of reinforced cage that was being lifted by a crane dislodged and fell about 30cm.

"No doubt this was something that shouldn't have occurred, but no-one was was injured and exclusion zones were put in place," Mr Wheelahan said.

The following day Ms Grace and then Minister for Transport and Main Roads Mark Bailey released a media statement stating they were seeking an urgent meeting with Nexus to see how "to improve safety on the project".

Mark Bailey was Queensland's minister for transport and main roads at the time of the incident. (AAP: Jono Searle)

Former project director Jose Sanchez and Ferrovial Agroman managing director Enrique Gomez attended the meeting the following day, February 23.

"The evidence you'll hear today from Mr Gomez and Mr Sanchez is when the ministers arrived and their meeting commenced, they say the ministers were not at all interested in the presentation put together by Nexus," Mr Wheelahan said.

"Rather, [Ms Grace] quickly took control of the meeting shortly after it commenced.

" [She] made it clear her expectation was Nexus would cooperate with the CFMEU, and was quite explicit, or the government would consider terminating their contract of the project. "

Mr Wheelahan said Mr Sanchez said in his statement this "was the sort of thing he expected to happen in a third world country … and not a developed country like Australia".

The inquiry heard soon after the meeting, Nexus withdrew its action with the Queensland Industrial Relations Commission — with that application believed by contractors to be behind the state government intervention.

title: NSW welcomes back superstar, hands Origin debut to Dragons gun
text:
NSW will welcome back superstar prop Millie Elliott after she missed the 2025 Women's State of Origin series while pregnant, and Emma Verran's pregnancy has opened the door for Teagan Berry to make her debut.

Elliott missed the 2025 NRLW season, but Origin I in Newcastle on April 30 is set to mark her first game since giving birth to daughter Gigi in September.

Meanwhile, Verran, who announced her pregnancy in January, has been replaced on the bench by Dragons fullback Berry.

Rampaging Roosters prop Rima Butler has also been named on the six-player bench for a potential debut.

NSW Women's Origin I team 1. Abbi Church (Parramatta Eels) 2. Jaime Chapman (Gold Coast Titans) 3. Jess Sergis (Sydney Roosters) 4. Isabelle Kelly (Sydney Roosters) 5. Jayme Fressard (Sydney Roosters) 6. Jocelyn Kelleher (Sydney Roosters) 7. Jesse Southwell (Brisbane Broncos) 8. Millie Elliott (Sydney Roosters) 9. Keeley Nizza (Sydney Roosters) 10. Ellie Johnston (Cronulla Sharks) 11. Tiana Penitani Gray (Cronulla Sharks) 12. Yasmin Meakes (Newcastle Knights) 13. Olivia Kernick (Sydney Roosters) 14. Olivia Higgins (Newcastle Knights) 15. Kennedy Cherrington (Parramatta Eels) 16. Kezie Apps (Wests Tigers) 17. Teagan Berry* (St George Illawarra Dragons) 18. Corban Baxter (Sydney Roosters) 19. Rima Butler* (Sydney Roosters) 20. Quincy Dodd (Cronulla Sharks) * Uncapped

"I'm really excited about the squad that we've named, it's a very well-balanced team from 1-17," coach John Strange said.

"The six-week training block was very enjoyable and bringing the players and staff together gave them a great opportunity to connect both on and off the field.

"I'm looking forward to camp starting and can't wait to get to work to defend the Shield."

Teagan Berry has been a constant presence at the back for the Dragons since her 2020 debut. (Getty Images: Scott Gardiner)

NSW won the series 2-1 last year, only losing the dead rubber third game.

Fourteen players from that series victory have been named for game one, although hooker Keeley Nizza (nee Davis) and back rower Yasmin Meakes (nee Clydsdale) will sport new names on the back of their jerseys.

Like Roosters teammate Boyle, Corban Baxter is also set to return to the Origin scene for the first time since 2024, named in jersey number 18 after missing last year due to injury.

Sharks captain Tiana Penitani-Gray adds a fourth starting position to her Origin career, named at back row after previously playing centre, wing and five-eighth for NSW.

Her move pushes former captain Kezie Apps to the bench.

Queensland named its team earlier on Wednesday, naming five potential debutants in the absence of star playmakers Ali Brigginshaw and Tarryn Aiken.

title: 'I cannot afford to lose': Moloney's slow path back to boxing summit
text:
Jason Moloney summed things up pretty succinctly ahead of his IBF Intercontinental bantamweight title fight against Andre Donovan, which is set to take place on Thursday night at Brisbane's Fortitude Music Hall.

"I cannot afford to lose to this guy," the 34-year-old said.

It was a statement said with all the usual hubris you'd expect from a pre-fight press conference but, for Moloney, it's not just hot air.

The simple truth is that as a boxer in his mid-30s, who has been at the very top of the sport since first challenging for a world title back in 2018, there is little room for error.

That fight, against Emmanuel Rodríguez in Florida, was the first of five world title contests he's been a part of in his 31-bout career, a career that saw him hold the WBO bantamweight title in 2023–24.

Jason Moloney (left) beat Vincent Astrolabio to win the WBO bantamweight title in 2023. (Getty Images: Top Rank Inc/Mikey Williams)

Prior to 2024, Moloney had only lost twice in his career — both times in world title fights, both times away from home, and both times against two superstars of the ring.

The defeat to two-time world champion and Olympic gold medallist Rodríguez was followed by becoming yet another one of four-weight world champion Naoya Inoue's victims.

But, following his stunning victory over Vincent Astrolabio to win the WBO belt in California, followed by a successful defence against Saul Sanchez in Canada, Moloney suffered back-to-back defeats in Japan against Yoshiki Takei and Tenshin Nasukawa.

Moloney, having battled his way through some of boxing's less glamorous locales to reach the summit of a division that lacks the glamour and attention that the bigger knockout artists can command, is now in that unenviable position of being trapped in the disintegrating scree on the other side, desperately scrabbling through the detritus of other former champions in a bid to upset the passage of time and return to the top.

"I feel really blessed to be here," Moloney said earlier this week.

"It feels bloody nice to see Moloney on the billboards again, first time I've headlined a show in quite a few years.

"To be given an opportunity to headline a card like this is something I'm really grateful for.

"I'm expecting a hard fight, but that's what we're in the game for, the hard fights that are going to push me, develop me, and get me back to another world title shot as soon as possible."

Jason Moloney has shared a ring with Naoya Inoue, one of the greatest fighters of this generation. (Getty Images: Top Rank Inc/Mikey Williams)

Moloney (28-4, 20KOs) has managed to gain a foothold, beating Herlan Gomez by fourth-round knock out on the Gold Coast last time out.

And now he hopes to take another step upwards against his latest opponent, a 12-2 (8KOs) American who will be hoping to deliver on his promise in what is his first overseas fight.

"You've had an amazing career," said the softly-spoken Donovan, who combines his fighting with working in an aluminium foundry in Ohio.

"I've seen a lot of you, I've followed your career.

"I'm a nobody. Your name under my belt is going to put me on that path to chase that world title that you're chasing right now.

"You've had a lovely career, but mine's only just starting."

Full fight card Jason Moloney vs Andre Donovan (bantamweight)

Ben Mahoney vs Dan Hill (super welterweight)

Max McIntyre vs Tej Pratap Singh (super middleweight)

Austin Aokuso vs Wulamu Tulake (light heavyweight)

Conor Wallace vs Walter Sequeira (light heavweight)

Xavier Fletcher vs Bongani Sibanda (middleweight)

Chris O'Reilly vs Joshua Hatherley (middleweight)

Suliaman Guushaa vs Scottie Williams (super welterweight)

This will be Moloney's second fight under Tasman Fighters, the promoter responsible for the rise of cruiserweight king Jai Opetaia (despite what the IBF seem to think) and heavyweight Justis Huni among others.

The partnership is one that promoter Mick Francis is looking forward to seeing develop over the next 18 months.

"This is a great fight," Francis said.

"Jason … this is one of Australia's all-time best fighters.

"He's 34 but he's got a new lease on life. I can see this guy fighting for another world title."

But first he'll have to get past Donovan.

"You asked for a war man and you've got it," the 29-year-old said.

"It was a long flight, I'm here now and I hope you're prepared.

"We might be the smallest guys on this card, but expect big fireworks."

Wallace left waiting but can't afford to slip up

Conor Wallace is waiting for his big chance. (Tasman Fighters: James Paul)

Speaking of the bigger men, the co-main event sees another man looking to raise himself up into a world title shot after a year of frustration and false promises left him stuck.

Light heavyweight Conor Wallace (16-1, 11KOs) has been inactive for 10 months, having been promised a fight against rising German star and IBF mandatory challenger, Michael Efert.

After much toing-and-froing however, Efert will take on unified champion and former undisputed title holder Dmitrii Bivol in Russia instead.

"That's the fight we were told that we were going to be getting and the one we were waiting on," Wallace said.

"We were listening to them [the IBF] and we were told we would be fighting Michael Eifert, then obviously the hold-up with Bivol…

"This is out of our control. We can only beat who they put in front of us.

"But trust me, I'll be ready when the time comes."

Conor Wallace is not afraid to bang. (Tasman Fighters: James Paul)

Francis acknowledged that Wallace's opponent, Argentine journeyman Walter Gabriel Sequeira (28-13-2, 19KOs), is a keep-busy contest for the Ireland-born Queenslander.

But anyone who has ever seen Wallace fight knows that it's unlikely to be dull.

"He can box, but the problem is, he wants to bang," Francis said of Wallace.

"He gives [coach] Steve Deller a heart attack every time he steps in the ring but he always gets the job done.

"He digs deep and that's why he's the best light heavyweight in the country.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

"Anyone who knows boxing knows the light heavyweight division is one of the toughest divisions out there … and this guy is number two in the IBF.

"That just goes to show how good [Wallace] is. We've got bigger things ahead for him."

Francis said Wallace should have been given a chance of a world title earlier than he will get one after Bivol "played the game" for six months, but said the entire team will just "go through the process".

Part of that process is to get another big win on home soil.

"We've got a good tough durable opponent … he's been in with a lot of tough guys, [British fighters] Lyndon Arthur, Anthony Yarde," Wallace said.

"This will be a good test, to see where I'm at against the likes of this guy, and we're looking overseas after this.

"This is a good fight for me, I'm not overlooking him, but I'll be making a statement and putting a highlight reel on."

title: Farmers fear potential mouse plague as CSIRO warns of high numbers
text:
If the cost of diesel and the lack of fertiliser were not enough, South Australia's farmers are also facing the prospect of a mouse plague.

With the traditional start to seeding coming up this weekend, growers around the state have been noticing an increase in the vermin.

"Probably within the last month and a half, we've started seeing the odd one around the sheds," said Matt Davey, who farms on the northern end of the Yorke Peninsula.

It is an area the CSIRO has projected will experience "high" mouse numbers.

According to the CSIRO's March house mouse forecast, activity has been high in parts of the state. (Supplied: CSIRO)

Mr Davey said mouse levels were already the worst he had seen in "four or five years".

"There's more moisture around, which has probably allowed them to breed up, because the last few years have been so dry," he said.

As part of ongoing research, the CSIRO has been monitoring mouse populations on the Adelaide Plains since late last year.

Research officer Steve Henry said the organisation had been seeing high numbers.

Steve Henry has been carrying out mouse bait trials on the Adelaide Plains. (ABC Rural: Isabella Kelly)

"We would be seeing something in the order of, in some sites, 100 to 200 mice per hectare," he said.

"In those sites where we're getting trap saturation, probably 400 to 600 mice per hectare."

No second chances

Mice are never good news, but the risks they pose at seeding time are especially significant.

"They'll just find the furrow, dig along, and eat the seed as you're putting it in," Mr Davey said.

"You get 'UFO rings' where around their holes there's just nothing because they've eaten the grain, so that can thin the crop pretty well.

"In plague levels, I've seen huge areas wiped out."

Matt Davey has started catching mice inside his family's home. (ABC Rural: Isabella Kelly)

Mr Henry said a plague was defined as 800 mice per hectare, but said farmers should already be taking action to prevent damage, including laying bait.

"After [the seed] is gone, the plant is gone. There's no replacing it," he said.

"And high-value crops like canola, we understand that seed is in short supply this year.

" So, if you lose a whole heap of your canola crop, you probably won't have the option to re-sow it. "

Kadina-based agronomist Nathaniel Lloyd, who works at agribusiness YP Ag, said there had been an uptick in the sale of mice controls.

"In the last couple of weeks we've started to really notice a lot more mice holes out in paddocks," Mr Lloyd said.

"Those numbers are starting to build up and it's becoming front of mind for our seeding program that we get control so that we can have good establishment of our crops.

The Davey family's newest recruit — Oreo — has been doing his bit in managing the mice. (ABC Rural: Isabella Kelly)

" It's just another problem to deal with. "

The business has been preparing for an increase in bait sales.

"We've started taking forward orders, getting in touch with our suppliers as well and making sure that they've got good stocks in their sheds so there's a smooth transition of bait from their sheds into ours, and then into farmers' alike," he said.

Baby boom

Mice can give birth to six to 10 babies every 19 to 21 days, so the paddocks where Mr Henry is catching 200 mice per hectare could soon be overrun.

"If you've got 100 females per hectare, it's only three weeks before you've got 600 mice per hectare," Mr Henry said.

Record-breaking rain earlier this year after more than two years of drought conditions could be a contributor to the increase, Mr Henry said.

"Historically, you tend to get high numbers of mice after more than 20 months of drier-than-average conditions, and then getting in-crop rainfall of more than 200 millimetres," he said.

"That seems to be the precursor that drives numbers up, and that's exactly what's happened in South Australia."

Steve Henry sounded the alarm late last year about a potential mouse plague. (ABC Rural: Isabella Kelly)

title: Charities struggle to stay open as food insecurity rises
text:
Food charities across Queensland say rising demand, tighter food supply and higher costs are making it harder to keep services running.

North of the Sunshine Coast, the pressure has forced one longstanding provider to close.

Recent national data suggests the strain is part of a broader trend exacerbated by household financial stress, soaring fuel prices and rising food insecurity.

'So many' requests for help

On Queensland's Sunshine Coast, Urban Angels delivers about 13,000 meals to vulnerable people each month.

Founder Birgit Sowden said demand for meals had increased significantly since December, with "so many new requests for help".

Birgit Sowden and Marta Reffo visit volunteers in the meal packing room. (ABC News: Janel Shorthouse)

"Sometimes they cry when we deliver to them — they just break down because it's so overwhelming," Ms Sowden said.

Jenny, who asked for her last name to be withheld for privacy reasons, is among those relying on Urban Angels' support while raising her grandson in a Sunshine Coast caravan park.

"I don't know what I'd do without them," she said.

Jenny says Urban Angels' meals help her feed and care for her grandson. (Supplied: Urban Angles)

"It's fantastic … you don't know how much it means to me."

Jenny said the groceries helped stretch meals at home.

"When they bring apples, I make apple pancakes and apple pie for my grandson," she said.

"And when I get vegetables like spinach, I hide them in meals so he'll eat them without knowing."

Jenny's household is not alone.

The national Foodbank Hunger Report 2025 found that about one in three households experienced some level of food insecurity in the past year, largely driven by cost-of-living pressures.

In the state's north, 43 per cent of Townsville households were deemed "food insecure" last year, a Foodbank Queensland report showed — with regional areas generally seeing higher rates than cities.

In Cairns, Mission Australia's Hambledon House said emergency food relief requests were "dramatically rising", with more "working poor" families seeking help as they juggled the cost of rent and groceries — adding pressure to already stretched community services.

A 2025 State of the Sector report by the Queensland Council of Social Service (QCOSS) found 80 per cent of organisations that helped communities reported a significant rise in demand over the past year, with 90 per cent unable to meet current needs.

The national charity regulator agreed.

"We're hearing consistently from charities that demand for essential services … continues to rise," Australian Charities and Not-for-profits Commission commissioner Sue Woodward said.

"Sometimes faster than what charities can keep up with."

The commissioner said, "Smaller charities [are] not faring as well when it comes to revenue, donations … and paid staff."

An Urban Angels meal aims to fill more than just empty tummies, says Birgit Sowden. (ABC News: Janel Shorthouse)

Charities stretched

For Urban Angels, rising ingredient and operating costs have forced the charity to cut output by 2,000 meals a month.

"But we won't turn anyone away," Ms Sowden said.

She said, despite the growing demand, people should not feel discouraged from seeking help.

But at Gympie, about 80 kilometres north of the Sunshine Coast, rising costs proved too much for food charity Victory Care, which has closed its doors after 16 years.

"Even petrol … just the [transport, supply and operating] costs were too much for the local church," Pastor George Miller said.

The service, run by Victory Church, closed in January.

Mr Miller said 300 vulnerable people in the region would be without a regular source of food hampers and emergency support.

"In some towns, there are other charities, but in Gympie, there's nowhere else," he said.

Charity groups are banding together to help each other. (from left) Quinton Litchfield and Tom Lew from Gateway Care, with Allan and Marrisa Cowburn from Life Care. (Supplied: Gateway Care)

Further west at Mundubbera in the North Burnett region, Life Care's food pantry was also struggling to stay open.

"The cost of providing for people in need has risen," Pastor Alan Cowburn said.

"Grants out here and government funding are very difficult to secure," he said.

He said assistance from Gateway Care was helping them stay afloat for now.

Fruit and vegetables are sorted into boxes at Gateway Care, ready for families in need on the Sunshine Coast. (Supplied: Gateway Care)

Funding status

Gateway Care, which reported helping more than 40,000 families over 25 years on the Sunshine Coast, had been hopeful of securing government funding to continue helping the community.

Managing director Tom Lew said after recording significant losses in 2024 due to changes to supermarket supply and rising costs, a $5 million pre-election LNP funding commitment in the same year was highly anticipated.

But, he said, "Here we are in 2026, and we haven't seen a cracker."

In 2024, Deputy Premier Jarrod Bleijie and Caloundra MP Kendall Morton made a pre-election "pinky promise" to a Gateway Care visitor at the funding announcement. (Supplied: Gateway Care)

A spokesperson for Queensland's Department of Families, Seniors, Disability Services and Child Safety said work to deliver the previously announced $5 million funding commitment to Gateway Care was "ongoing".

The spokesperson said the department recognised that increased demand for food assistance was being reported across Queensland.

They said $3.57 million over two years from 2024-25 had strengthened SecondBite and OzHarvest food relief capacity statewide, and $2.88 million over four years had supported Foodbank Queensland infrastructure upgrades.

title: The Michael Jackson biopic is as generic as its subject was singular
text:
There was — and is — no-one like Michael Jackson.

Nearly 17 years after his death at age 50 in 2009, the pop superstar's music and art continues to electrify, even as his unprecedented fame, shrouded in rumour and unsettling allegations, confounds and mystifies.

Fast facts about Michael What: The rise of pop superstar Michael Jackson, from his musical beginnings in the 1960s to the heights of his 1980s fame Starring: Jaafar Jackson, Colman Domingo, Nia Long, Kendrick Sampson, Miles Teller, Juliano Krue Valdi Director: Antoine Fuqua Where: In cinemas April 23 Likely to make you feel: Entertained — and frustrated

He's been exalted and excoriated, deified and dragged through the mud, and yet — like the shapeshifting trickster of Remember the Time — he remains elusive, a contradiction whose truth, whatever it may be, will likely never be known.

There's little doubt that the slick, celebratory new biopic Michael — produced under the aegis of the Jackson estate and shepherded by Bohemian Rhapsody impresario Graham King — is a careful exercise in brand management from a robust $US2 billion enterprise.

That it's been beset by legally mandated reshoots and resistance from both Jackson's daughter Paris and his superstar sister Janet (who opted out of being portrayed on-screen) only raises more questions over the project's intent.

But while the media would have you believe the movie is an attempt to rehabilitate a tarnished image, anyone who's been paying attention knows the resurgent Jackson hardly needs the PR makeover; his monthly Spotify streams alone sit at 68 million, far surpassing any so-called legacy artist.

1982's irrepressible, world-conquering smash Thriller continues to capture listeners barely conceived when he died. His influence on modern pop, from The Weeknd to BTS, is everywhere.

Michael cuts the King of Pop's story short, ending around the time of his 1988 Bad tour. (Supplied: Universal)

Jackson's enduring popularity is almost unthinkable in the wake of 2019's Leaving Neverland, a harrowing if one-sided film in which two men alleged Jackson had abused them as children.

Mainstream media — for whom the star has long been a reliably lucrative freak-show — fell over themselves to endorse it, but the music proved more powerful.

Michael, the movie, can only gesture at such paradox, but it also faces a more significant hurdle: how to effectively dramatise a life lived almost entirely in front of the camera — a life that Jackson, with his media savvy and visual artistry, fashioned into his own, real-time mythology?

Unfolding at a breakneck clip, the movie certainly wastes no time playing the hits.

It picks up in 1966, in a Dickensian-looking Gary, Indiana, with eight-year-old Michael (a dynamite Juliano Krue Valdi) and his brothers literally whipped into musical shape by taskmaster patriarch Joseph (Colman Domingo, valiantly battling a two-dimensional cartoon), all while saintly mother Katherine (Nia Long) looks on.

Newcomer Juliano Krue Valdi (centre) features as a young Michael, during his time in the Jackson 5. (Supplied: Universal)

Christened the Jackson 5, the boys dance for pennies on the Chitlin' Circuit, find Motown stardom with their miraculous early hits, and — faster than one of his signature spins — the emergent solo Michael (played as an adult by Jaafar Jackson) is recording his breakthrough record Off the Wall with Quincy Jones (Kendrick Sampson), welcoming Bubbles the chimp to his burgeoning menagerie at the Encino family home, and plotting world domination with Thriller.

Doing an uncanny impersonation of his uncle, Jaafar Jackson — who shares Michael's lithe moves and his contagious smile — gives a warm, sometimes soulful performance, at least to the extent that the film permits him to stretch dramatically.

Whenever he's performing — which is often — the movie finds its transformative charge by keeping the focus on the music, which remains among the wonders of the pop world.

Experiencing the isolated vocals of songs like Who's Lovin' You, I Want You Back and Don't Stop 'Til You Get Enough is almost worth the ticket price alone. At the same time, the film struggles to find a dramatic point of view on its subject — let alone anything resembling insight.

Before the reshoots, the muscular action filmmaker Antoine Fuqua (Training Day) had bookended his story with a 1993 police raid on Jackson's Neverland estate, a bold gambit that engaged directly with the first round of allegations and positioned the film as a reclamation — however controversial — of the star's legacy.

Trailer for Michael Jackson biopic released a year behind schedule — with a glaring gap Antoine Fuqua's movie was meant to be released months ago, but was delayed twice because of reshoots and eventually pushed back to April 2026 — a whole year behind schedule.

Minus that dramatic sauce, the film plays as a series of polished montages that barely summon a through-line, thanks in part to a screenplay (from Gladiator scribe John Logan) that's riddled with biopic cliche — and a bizarre self-insert from Jackson's estate lawyer John Branca, played by Miles Teller, as the unsung hero of the movie.

Fatally, the film leans into a standard daddy-issues drama: Joseph berates and beats Michael, and Michael retreats into a childlike fantasy world of toys and stuffed animals that foreshadows his future home.

It's not that it isn't significant — as an adult, Michael would speak of how his father's presence would cause him to throw up — rather the movie doesn't know what to do with any of it.

In one starkly fumbled scene, a bandaged Michael returns home from the plastic surgeon — minus the nose for which Joseph had taunted him — and encounters his father in a hallway. It's a charged moment that goes nowhere.

Oscar-nominated actor Colman Domingo steps into the shoes of Joseph Jackson, Michael's domineering father and manager. (Supplied: Universal)

Similarly, the seamier side of child stardom — the darker intimations of abuse, the adult world to which young Michael was prematurely exposed — are absent.

"I was far too young to grasp the meanings of most of the words in these songs," Jackson wrote in his 1988 autobiography Moonwalk.

Is it any wonder the pint-sized child-man, thrust into performing lover-man soul as grade-schooler, would pursue an adult life of childlike wonder?

Sure, he composes Beat It after watching a gang report on TV and Thriller while deep in the throes of horror movie fandom, but what psychic demons drove him to the breathtaking ambiguity of Billie Jean, or the exhilarating, multi-faceted exhortations of Wanna Be Startin' Somethin'?

Jackson's desire to break music's racial barrier, meanwhile, is paid perfunctory service; by the time the movie gets to his battle to have his videos played on MTV, it's relegated to a cameo that's practically contemptuous in its in-joke winking.

Michael recreates the filming of the Thriller music video, which referenced multiple horror movies. (Supplied: Universal)

"I have to shine my light," asserts Michael at one point, "spread love and joy, to heal — that's my destiny".

But at what cost to himself, and to those around him? What of the increasingly paranoid superstar trapped in fame's gilded cage, for whom James Baldwin once wrote — at the height of Thriller's cultural and commercial success — "He will not swiftly be forgiven for having turned so many tables"?

Only occasionally does the movie evoke a sense of Jackson's immense loneliness, an isolation that led him to create the fantasy world that would be his undoing.

Ironically, it's the film's abrupt closing sequence that finally teases something vital.

Intended as a victory lap for the emancipated solo star, the recreation of Jackson's 1988 Bad performance bristles with defiance and rising aggression.

Michael leaves us with an unreadable image of its subject, his eyes hidden behind an outstretched hand, his mouth locked in a hardened grimace, as though readying himself for a turbulent future to come.

Perhaps it's just as Jackson himself would have wanted — to remain a mystery.

Michael is in cinemas now.

title: Aussie junior 'obsessed' with golf set to test his skills overseas
text:
Alby Weyden's parents say he was two years old and in nappies when he first became obsessed with golf balls.

Four years on, he has been invited to the US Kids Golf Canadian Invitational — a top-tier international tournament designed for youth development.

His father, Jason Weyden, said he was shocked when his son received the invitation with a little over two months to prepare for the July competition.

"It's exciting for everyone, we certainly weren't expecting it," Mr Weyden said.

Jason Weyden is Alby's caddy in tournaments, providing him guidance on what clubs to use. (Supplied: Emma Weyden)

If Alby plays well, he could qualify for other major championships, gaining the chance to showcase his skills in front of golfing scouts and sponsors.

His mother, Emma Weyden, said their son's love for the game developed before he could walk.

Emma Weydon said Alby would carry a container of 20 golf balls with him everywhere they went. (Supplied: Emma Weyden)

"He would carry a plastic container with 20 golf balls in it and every time we went to the golf store, he'd always want a new pack of balls," Ms Weyden said.

"He would sleep with those golf balls next to him, and he would shower with them.

"Other kids were taking stuffed toys to daycare — he was taking golf balls."

Watching his father in the shadows of a driving range, the two-year-old grew curious.

"We started him off with some lessons when he was three, but he never really had to be taught things," Ms Weyden said.

"He just knew what to do."

Financial hurdles

The family estimates it will cost them around $15,000 for flights, accommodation, playing fees and other expenses to attend the tournament on short notice.

Ms Weyden said the family would struggle to fund the trip.

"We're a single-income family, and I'm at home, homeschooling my daughter due to medical reasons, and my husband is a mechanic," Ms Weyden said.

"It's not going to be easy.

" What kind of mum would I be if I didn't do everything to try to get him to these events? "

The mother-of-three said she had received some negative criticism from people online.

Emma Weyden said Alby became obsessed with the sport before he could walk. (ABC Sunshine Coast: Jake Kearnan)

"People ask me if I'm forcing him to play," she said.

" There are tantrums when we can't get him to the golf course — he wants to come every single day. "

Alby trains nearly every day and has one-on-one coaching lessons.

While other kids were taking stuffed toys to daycare, Alby would take his beloved golf balls. (Supplied: Emma Weyden)

Sports psychologist Tacey Veivers said it was a good thing the Weyden family supported their son's dreams.

"We don't want parents fearing that they're going to be judged if they do help a talented child to pursue that sport," Ms Veivers said.

"If a child is showing interest in something, there is no better thing for a parent to do than to facilitate that interest."

She said the criticism shone a light on a wider judgement issue in junior sport.

"A lot of those experiences playing sport will help a child to develop confidence, a self-esteem and an understanding of commitment."

Local champ

The six-year-old was recently crowned the US Kids Golf Local Tour Champion on the Sunshine Coast for boys aged six and under.

It is one of five tournaments held across the country.

Alby Weyden recently became a US Kids Golf Local Tour champion for boys aged six and under. (Supplied: Emma Weyden)

Armed with his favourite nine iron, Alby said he enjoyed the social side of the sport.

"I like playing at ranges, and I like playing competitions with my friends," he said.

"I like playing at courses with my Dad.

"He helps me hit better shots."

Alby has been turning heads on the course and has challenged a few adults as well.

"I beat my Grandad the last time I played golf with him," he said.

Mr Weyden is Alby's caddy during tournaments and said he was amazed by how composed his son was on the fairway.

"The hardest part is trying to keep myself calm, I get butterflies every time he hits a shot," Mr Weyden said.

Aside from Canada, Hawaii is also on Alby's list of countries he would like to play in. (Supplied: Emma Weyden)

"It doesn't bother him if he has a bad shot or a bad hole; he just has so much enjoyment for the game and loves being out there.

"He always has a smile on his face and focuses on the next shot."

title: Woman's life 'changed for the better' after GPs in SA trained to diagnose ADHD
text:
When Adelaide resident Tayla Giles found out that specially trained GPs in South Australia could diagnose and treat attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), she booked an appointment almost immediately.

"I was just amazed, blown away. It was something I could finally access," she said.

She had her initial assessment appointment with a GP in March, and after about a month, she got an ADHD diagnosis and is now receiving treatment.

"It was really smooth, unexpectedly easy. Basically, I called up, booked in the appointment," she said.

"There was about a two-week wait, and then in the waiting room I had to fill in a pre-appointment survey, and then when I went in with the GP it was like an hour-long interview. It was very thorough."

Ms Giles had been on a waiting list for eight months to see a psychiatrist for an ADHD assessment, which was going to cost about $2,000.

By comparison, it cost her about $600 to get the diagnosis through a GP.

Tayla Giles says the process to get an ADHD assessment was "smooth". (ABC News: Lincoln Rothall)

"And that was across three appointments, the whole total was $600," she said.

"It [my life] has changed for the better, it's been incredible. I feel like I know myself so much more now, I have such a deeper understanding of how my mind works. I'm not just going to be this way forever; there's more to it than that.

" And I think more than ever I am kinder to myself, I have more empathy and understanding and patience for myself. It's been amazing. "

At the end of February this year, 100 GPs across South Australia became qualified to diagnose, treat and prescribe medication for ADHD in adults and children eight years old and above — with hopes of another 100 coming on board by the end of the year.

Ms Giles said it was her psychologist who sent her the list of 100 GPs with the training, and she was thrilled to see her regular GP was one of them.

"It was something I had thought about for a long time, but my psychologist wasn't able to diagnose, so I jumped on the opportunity," she said.

Dr Sian Goodson says there are long wait lists for GPs who are qualified to diagnose ADHD. (ABC News: Sarah Maunder)

The chair of the South Australian branch of the Royal Australian College of General Practitioners (RACGP), Sian Goodson, said there was an overwhelming demand for GPs to have the ability to diagnose and treat ADHD, and the program has been popular so far.

"Some GPs are seeing patients they've known for years and are now confirming that diagnosis, other GPs have opened their books for new patients and reporting huge amounts of interest," Dr Goodson said.

"GPs are getting long wait lists already in some cases for patients wanting to access those diagnostic services, so huge demand out there, and we need more GPs to be able to do this work, so we can see more patients."

Limited services in regions

This week, the ABC's Four Corners program released data showing ADHD diagnoses across Australia have surged over the past eight years, but that the rates of diagnosis vary by location.

Experts interviewed by Four Corners agree that the prevalence of ADHD in Australian adults is between 2.5 and 3 per cent.

The neighbourhoods where ADHD rates are skyrocketing ADHD diagnoses in adults have exploded across Australia since 2017. For the first time, we have the data that shows where it is — and isn't — happening.

In South Australia, all areas are below that prevalence rate, but the highest areas are in Gawler and Two Wells at 1.99 per cent, and Mitcham at 1.888 per cent.

But the rates are far lower in regional areas, such as the Outback North and East (0.81 per cent), Eyre Peninsula and the South West (0.82 per cent) and the Murray Mallee (0.9 per cent).

On the Eyre Peninsula, there are no GPs who can diagnose ADHD in adults.

There are limited options for psychiatrists, with many only offering telehealth services.

Jasmine Calderwood lives in Port Lincoln and believes she may have ADHD.

Jasmine Calderwood hopes a local service would provide ADHD support. (Supplied)

She got a referral to a telehealth service to get a diagnosis, but due to the costs involved and a need to prioritise her daughter's health needs, she has yet to begin that process.

She said if there were a local specialist or GP qualified to provide ADHD support, it would be a lot easier for her.

"There's a lot of planning that goes in place as well, and if you had something that's locally accessible, I feel like you're more likely to follow through with it," she said.

"I think if it was just here, you can just have an appointment and you can just go, rather than having to do everything via telehealth."

Of the 100 GPs that began diagnosing and treating ADHD at the end of February, Dr Goodson said about 40 per cent of those were based in regional areas of SA.

"We had over 300 GPs apply for 100 places for the initial cohort, and we tried to pick GPs from across the state and across the regions," she said.

The RACGP is hoping another 100 GPs will be trained in ADHD treatment and diagnosis by the end of the year.

"And we're really focusing on that second 100 of getting GPs in areas where's there not many services available to patients, so we'll prioritising regional/rural areas, and really trying to get a spread of GPs across the state," Dr Goodson said.

SA's new Health Minister Blair Boyer said he understood there was high demand for ADHD services and re-affirmed the government's commitment to those extra 100 GPs.

"We have to do that, but I'm also conscious that it's not just about providing what you might describe as your 'normal' GP services, but what can we do as a government to encourage those GPs perhaps to upskill themselves so the array of diagnoses and treatment that they can offer for in rural, regional and remote parts of the state is greater than it would normally be," he said.

SA Health chief medical officer Michael Cusack said having specifically trained GPs would helped South Australians "receive a faster diagnosis, shorter wait times and great access to important care".

title: Three weeks' pay guaranteed for Liberty Bell Bay smelter workers
text:
Wages for workers at the Liberty Bell Bay Smelter have been secured for the next three weeks, in a deal reached between the Tasmanian and federal governments.

Australia's last ferromanganese smelter, at Bell Bay in Northern Tasmania, was put into administration on March 23 this year, and a search for a new buyer continues.

Union members from Liberty Bell Bay meet with union representatives outside the smelter to call on state and federal support with only one more guaranteed paycheck next Friday. (ABC News: Kelsey Reid)

Last week, the near 200 strong workforce had been told to take unpaid leave from this Friday, or lose their jobs.

Leave without pay, or lose your job — decision day for smelter workers With Australian and international bidders apparently eyeing off the troubled Liberty Bell Bay smelter, the workers and their families just want to know they'll have a job at the end of it all.

Federal Industry Minister Tim Ayres visited the site with Premier Jeremy Rockliff this morning to announce a deal had been reached to provide the surety for three weeks of workers' wages.

The loan, worth $3 million, will be split evenly between the two governments.

Premier Jeremy Rockliff said, "We need to give the sale process the best possible chance."

Administrators from Ernst & Young have indicated there are a dozen potential buyers for Liberty Bell Bay, and say a sale could take two to six months.

Local industry groups and unions say the smelter remains a profitable business in "the right hands", but that retaining the highly skilled workforce remains key to a deal.

The Australian Workers' Union assistant national secretary, Chris Donovan, said the fortnightly wage bill for the site was about $1.6 million.

Deal reached as deadline for workers gets pushed back

Union members from Liberty Bell Bay meet with union representatives outside the smelter to call on state and federal support with only one more guaranteed paycheck next Friday. (ABC News: Kelsey Reid)

Workers were originally told they needed to decide if they would leave their jobs by 5pm on Tuesday.

However, with the state and federal governments nearing a deal, that deadline was extended until Thursday.

Asked why a deal had taken so long, Senator Ayres said the smelter entering administration was an "extraordinary development", and that there were "uncertain circumstances".

"Nobody has been sitting on their hands here — everybody has been focused on achieving the right outcome for this community," he said.

Mr Donovan said the AWU would have liked to have seen action sooner, and that there was "great uncertainty".

"Could the result have been better? Yes, it could have, but we are happy with where we are now," he said.

"We are hopeful for longer-term support, and the successful sale and ongoing operation of Liberty Bell Bay," he said.

He said the union would continue to heap pressure on the state and federal government for longer-term support.

Mr Rockliff did not close the door on further support in three weeks if a buyer was not found.

"We'll continue communicating with the administrator and the workforce — we've demonstrated today that we're in their corner," he said.

Troubled period for smelter

Liberty Bell Bay is Australia's last producer of ferromanganese alloy, which is used to strengthen steel.

Liberty Bell Bay enters voluntary administration The owner of Australia's only manganese alloy smelter has entered voluntary administration. It means further uncertainty for the Tasmanian smelter's workforce.

Today's announcement follows an extended period of uncertainty under the ownership of British businessman Sanjeev Gupta and his company GFG Alliance, which purchased the smelter in 2019.

It has been in care and maintenance mode since last May, with GFG Alliance citing issues with iron ore supply and uncertain market conditions.

Last year, the state government lent GFG Alliance $20 million to purchase iron ore, which has sat unused at the Bell Bay site.

Incoming government briefings obtained by the ABC show that GFG Alliance has moved about $200 million out of Liberty Bell Bay to related parties via inter-company loans and paid a cash dividend to the UK parent company in 2022.

The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) has filed action in the New South Wales Supreme Court to wind up Liberty Bell Bay, alleging it failed to lodge annual reports for the financial years ending in 2021, 2022, 2023 and 2024.

Contractors to the site are also believed to be owed millions of dollars.

Liberty Bell Bay is Australia's only remaining ferromanganese smelter. (GFG Alliance)

Mr Rockliff said the "owners have let us down, big time".

"That's an absolute understatement," he said.

" They've deserted the community and deserted the workforce, and showed absolute disrespect for Tasmania, for Northern Tasmania and the workers. "

Mr Ayres agreed with the criticism.

"The person who is squarely responsible for all of those debts that have been incurred, for letting down this community, is the bloke who ran GFG," he said.

title: 'How many beers have you had?' Flood evacuees claim racial profiling
text:
Evacuees from remote Northern Territory communities say they are being treated "like prisoners" by security guards at emergency accommodation in Batchelor, set up after catastrophic flooding forced them from their homes last month.

Homes inundated and locals band together: NT floods in pictures Many regions across the Northern Territory were submerged by flooding through March this year. Here is some of the most dramatic footage from the extreme weather event, right across the Top End.

Residents from Palumpa and Daly River / Nauiyu have been living at the Batchelor Institute of Indigenous Tertiary Education, south of Darwin, for the past month, with private security guards stationed at the entry and exit.

Daly River resident James Parry, who has been living in emergency accommodation since March, said evacuees felt racially profiled when coming and going.

"If you walk down to the store and come back, the first thing they ask you is 'How many beers have you had today?'" he said.

"You try and tell them 'I haven't been at the pub' and that's where the conflict starts.

" They're treating us like prisoners, it's terrible. "

Mr Parry said the security guards searched evacuees' cars and bags every time someone exited or entered the premises, leaving residents feeling vulnerable.

James Parry says security guards are routinely searching evacuees bags and cars. (ABC News: Pete Garnish)

"You sign back in, your car gets searched — everybody gets told to get out of the car, and they search your bag and your private stuff, which is not right," he said.

Another resident, who did not want to be named, shared similar experiences.

"They're invading our privacy and we're not doing anything," she said.

" Even if they just let you out for like five, 10 minutes in the morning, they still ask you if you're a resident or if you have alcohol in the car — who serves alcohol at 8 o'clock in the morning? "

She said as the weeks went on, fewer people were attending meetings convened by the centre.

One resident sheltering at the evacuation hub shared their story with the ABC on the condition of anonymity. (ABC News: Pete Garnish)

"Everyone turns up expecting to hear some good news about home, but we hear little about home and more about the rules and what not to do around here," she said.

In a statement, a spokesperson said the NT government took concerns raised by evacuees seriously and the measures in place were designed to ensure safety.

"Security measures are in place to maintain a safe, respectful and alcohol-free environment, and may include bag or vehicle checks on entry," they said.

“These measures are designed to protect residents and staff and to ensure the site remains safe for everyone."

Aboriginal organisations snubbed

Aboriginal Peak Organisations NT (APONT) offered to send volunteers to assist NT government workers at the evacuation centre more than a week ago, but says it received no response.

Late last week, the NT government instead announced students on placement from Charles Darwin University's social work undergraduate degree would assist at Batchelor.

The emergency accommodation was established in Batchelor after last month's floods to support evacuees still unable to return home. (ABC News: Pete Garnish)

Speaking at a meeting of flood evacuees outside the centre on Monday, Northern Land Council (NLC) chair Matthew Ryan questioned the NT government's logic.

"We need to engage the right people in the community, which is the APONT group," he said.

"Why aren't we utilising them to interpret and to work with the community closely?

" Why get university students who'll have problems understanding my people? "

Mr Ryan said NLC staff had routinely been barred from entering the site, even after pre-arranging their visits, a claim backed up by Aboriginal Housing NT chief executive Leeanne Caton.

Matthew Ryan says Northern Land Council staff have been blocked from entering the evacuation centre. (ABC News: Pete Garnish)

"I went down to the centre [on Monday] and I was asked if I was from the NLC," she said.

"I said 'No, I'm not', and I was allowed to go in, but apparently if you're the NLC you need to give 24 hours' notice before you can come down here — what's going on?"

After six weeks at the centre, one resident said she was disheartened by the lack of access for the land council.

"They're keeping everybody in the dark," she said.

" Are they afraid that NLC are going to say something they don't want everyone to know? "

The NT government spokesperson said it was aware of correspondence offering support and would continue to work with Aboriginal-controlled organisations.

In response to the NLC's access claims, the spokesperson said security decisions were made to protect residents' privacy.

"We are balancing requests from organisations with feedback from residents, who have told us they want to limit the number of visitors on site during this time," they said.

"Where onsite access is not appropriate, alternative options have been offered, including facilitating meetings with residents at offsite locations."

Uncertainty over Palumpa return plan

Evacuees from Palumpa told a meeting of stakeholders they had questions about a plan to repatriate some members of the remote Indigenous community from as soon as Wednesday.

Community members say they were told about the development on Monday morning, having previously believed they would be staying in Batchelor for several more weeks.

Residents living at the shelter want more transparency regarding repatriation plans. (ABC News: Pete Garnish)

While many have been longing to return home, the announcement has raised concerns about the liveability of the community when they get back.

"It's a health and safety issue, there could be mould build-up within the houses because of the moisture and the wet season," Mr Ryan said.

"Food security, what's that going to look like?"

Ms Caton said the repatriation process had been marred by poor communication.

Leeanne Caton says Palumpa residents were surprised to hear about the latest repatriation plan. (ABC News: Pete Garnish)

"There is no flow of communication to these people," she said.

"We heard [yesterday] that Palumpa residents might be going back on Wednesday, well that was news to them."

Arnhem Land Progress Association chief executive Alastair King said he was surprised by Monday's repatriation announcement, having recently assessed the damage at Palumpa.

Remote community's bid to relocate progresses after '30 years of asking' Flood-affected residents from Daly River are in talks with the NT government about moving the community to higher ground, with a land donation offered up.

"The store has been a metre under water, all of the stock is gone, the chiller freezer's all turned to liquid, so that's a biohazard and has to be disposed of," he said.

"The store manager's house had water three-quarters of the way up the walls, so that whole house has got to be gutted because of the black mould."

Mr King said if residents were to return to the community in coming days, there would be serious limitations on what goods and services could be delivered.

"There's no clinic, no school, no essential services except for power, water and sewer," he said.

The NT government spokesperson said provision for the delivery of school, health and store services in Palumpa had been established, and the government had consulted with elders prior to the announcement.

They said the repatriation was still expected to go ahead today, subject to final safety checks.

"Returns will happen in stages, starting with residents whose homes are ready and safe," they said.

title: NDIS announcement LIVE updates: Mark Butler reveals long-anticipated reforms to $50b scheme in National Press Club speech
text:
The NDIS was originally intended to support around 410,000 people with a disability, the health minister has told the National Press Club.

Today there are 760,000 people on the scheme. While new eligibility rules need to be worked through, our initial modelling will see the number of people on the scheme reduce to around 600,000 by the end of the decade instead of growing to well over 900,000. This is still substantially higher than the Productivity Commission projection of 550,000 by the end of the decade.”

This would suggest about 160,000 people will transition out of the scheme once the new eligibility rules come into effect.

The new eligibility scheme will mean autistic adults could be kicked off the scheme based on their support needs.

Butler said autistic children aged under nine would be included in the Thriving Kids program that is being developed, but others would be based on a new objective assessment tool.

title: Marrickville Pork Roll
text:
Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime.

“This is the original and the best Marrickville Pork Roll,” says Nga Du, who co-founded the banh mi shop in a former toilet block with husband Khiem Du in 2008, and moved to a larger space next door in 2021.

Their Vietnamese pork sandwiches are consistently ranked among the city’s best – the white bread rolls are baked fresh (crunchy on the outside, soft on the inside, so large it’s almost intimidating) then slathered with rich pâté and stacked with cold-cut pork, pickled vegetables and fresh salad.

Lengthy weekend queues led to a further four venues opening across Sydney, but Du considers this one, where she works, special: “Because I love it, I put my whole heart into it,” she says. The crackling pork belly is her pick, but all options remain affordable, priced between $8 to $11. Once your sandwich is in-hand, head to the original shopfront with its faded red awning to buy a fresh sugarcane juice from what is now 236 Juice Bar.
